name: Hecht, Heiko
recent_1: Kehl, L., Brietzke, A., Pham Xuan, R., & Hecht, H. (2026). Anticipatory vibrotactile cues about upcoming turns reduce motion sickness: A study with car passengers on public roads. Applied Ergonomics, 135, 104773. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104773
recent_1_synopsis: In a counterbalanced within-subjects field study, 40 passengers took two 30-minute rides on public roads while watching a movie, with seat-integrated actuators delivering anticipatory vibrotactile cues about upcoming left and right turns roughly one second before each maneuver. The cues significantly reduced reported carsickness (Fast Motion Sickness Scale), demonstrating that lateral-maneuver pre-warning alone is an effective real-world countermeasure. The work extends the team's motion-sickness mitigation research into naturalistic, on-road conditions.
recent_2: Kehl, L., Brietzke, A., Pham Xuan, R., & Hecht, H. (2026). Anticipatory vibrotactile cues mitigate motion sickness in car passengers: an implementation study on public roads. Displays, 91, 103195. DOI: 10.1016/j.displa.2025.103195
recent_2_synopsis: This implementation study tested a seat-based vibrotactile cueing system that signaled upcoming turns and acceleration/deceleration events about 0.7 seconds in advance during 30-minute public-road rides with 39 participants in a within-subjects design. Anticipatory cues significantly mitigated car sickness, and the authors show that actuators embedded in the car seat are a practical channel for conveying such cues. It demonstrates feasibility of deploying anticipatory haptic countermeasures in real vehicles for automated/passenger driving.
recent_3: Michalareas, G., Lehr, C., Grabenhorst, M., & Hecht, H. (2025). Social processing distorts physical distance perception. Scientific Reports, 15(1). DOI: 10.1038/s41598-025-89935-9
recent_3_synopsis: This study examines how social-cognitive processing biases the perception of physical interpersonal distance, linking Hecht's longstanding interest in personal-space and space perception to social factors. The findings indicate that the social meaning of another person systematically distorts judged physical distance rather than distance being read off purely from optical/geometric cues. It connects spatial perception with social cognition in a high-visibility open-access venue.
seminal_1: Keshavarz, B., & Hecht, H. (2011). Validating an Efficient Method to Quantify Motion Sickness. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 53(4), 415-426. DOI: 10.1177/0018720811403736
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced and validated the Fast Motion Sickness Scale (FMS), a single-item verbal scale that allows continuous, low-burden tracking of motion sickness severity during an experiment. With roughly 870 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is Hecht's most-cited work and has become a standard measurement instrument across motion-sickness, simulator-sickness, and cybersickness research. Its foundational status comes from giving the field a fast, reliable, and easily administered alternative to lengthy post-hoc questionnaires, published in Human Factors itself.
seminal_2: Hecht, H., Vogt, S., & Prinz, W. (2001). Motor learning enhances perceptual judgment: a case for action-perception transfer. Psychological Research, 65(1), 3-14. DOI: 10.1007/s004260000043
seminal_2_synopsis: This study showed that practicing a timed motor action improves subsequent perceptual judgments of that same action, providing evidence for bidirectional action-perception transfer. With approximately 260 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is among Hecht's most influential basic-science contributions and is widely cited in the common-coding / ideomotor and embodied-cognition literatures. It is foundational for demonstrating that perception and action draw on shared representations.
expertise_overview: Heiko Hecht is an experimental psychologist whose research centers on visual and spatial perception, including pictorial and 3D space perception, perceived room geometry, slant, and interpersonal/personal space. A major strand of his work addresses motion sickness, visually induced motion sickness, and cybersickness, including measurement methodology (the Fast Motion Sickness Scale) and countermeasures such as anticipatory vibrotactile and auditory cues for carsickness in automated and passenger driving. He also studies naive/intuitive physics, time-to-contact and arrival-time estimation, and the perception of dynamic events. A further theme is the coupling of perception and action (action-perception transfer, motor expertise effects on judgment). His applied human-factors interests include driving simulators, camera-monitor systems, and the acceptance and comfort of automated transport.
notes: High-confidence identification: Heiko Hecht, Chair of Experimental Psychology, Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz (Germany); perception, motion sickness, intuitive physics, time-to-contact. Disambiguated via the supplied verified Human Factors driving/perception papers plus matching topics and co-authors (Keshavarz, Kehl, von Castell). Sources: Google Scholar (user 0nKyLkIAAAAJ; ~9,024 total citations, h-index 53), JGU Mainz faculty/publication page, ResearchGate, ScienceDirect author page. All six DOIs (2 seminal, 3 recent, plus anchors) verified to resolve via Crossref API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles/authors/venues. Citation counts (seminal_1 ~870; seminal_2 ~260) are from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 and are approximate. Recent papers chosen to span both applied motion-sickness work (Applied Ergonomics 2026, Displays 2026) and basic perception (Scientific Reports 2025); note the two 2026 vibrotactile papers are companion field studies. No unverifiable DOIs were included; no DOIs were invented.
